items.name: Written pitch (may differ from sounded for transposing instruments)
Staff position override (display on specific line/space)
Ottava line continuation across system/page breaks
Ottava endpoint hook style (hook down, no hook, etc.)
Percussion map (mapping staff positions to/from sounds)
Slash notation on pitched staves (rhythmic slashes)
Percussion notehead type per instrument (x, triangle, diamond, etc.)
Scoop (upward approach into note from below)
Plop (downward approach into note from above)
Concert pitch reference frequency (A=440, A=415, etc.)
Scordatura display (notation shows fingered pitch vs. sounding)
Custom tuning system definition (just intonation, Pythagorean, etc.)
Sharp + sharp (visual alternative to double sharp)
Flat + flat (visual alternative to double flat)
Turkish comma accidentals (koma, bakiye, küçük mücenneb, etc.)
Whether accidental is displayed (force show / force hide)
Ficta accidental (editorial accidental in early music)
Courtesy accidental on pitch restated after barline
Courtesy accidental after key signature change
Courtesy accidental when same pitch class altered in another octave
Courtesy accidental when same pitch altered in another voice
Courtesy accidental when same pitch altered on another staff
Courtesy accidental on note following a tied note
Courtesy accidental after first/second endings (volta brackets)
Accidental stacking order / graphical position of accidentals
Accidental carries through the measure (modern convention)
Accidental applies only to the specific octave
Accidental applies to all octaves of that pitch class
Accidental applies only to the single note (Second Viennese School convention)
Accidental canceled by key signature change
Grace note accidentals affecting subsequent main notes
Tie chain (multiple consecutive ties, e.g., whole note across three bars)
Explicit tie target identification (reference to end note)
Laissez vibrer (l.v.) tie (open-ended, no target note)
Incoming tie (no visible source, e.g., after repeat or system start)
Enharmonic ties (e.g., C♯ tied to D♭)
Tie split across system/page break (two half-arcs)
Multiple simultaneous ties in a chord
Partial chord ties (only some notes in chord tied)
Ties on adjacent seconds in a chord
Ties on unisons (same pitch in different voices)
Cross-staff ties (notes on different staves of same instrument)
Cross-voice ties (note tied into a different voice)
Tie endpoint positioning relative to notehead
Position/order of multiple stacked articulations on one note
Breath mark symbol (comma, tick, Salzedo, etc.)
Caesura placement (between notes vs. at barline)
Trill with accidental on auxiliary note
Trill line continuation across system/page break
Grace note timing (steal from previous vs. following note)
Slur from grace note to main note
Hairpin from niente (circle at closed end)
Hairpin to niente (circle at closed end)
Hairpin ending at a specific dynamic marking
Subito hairpin (immediate change, no gradual wedge)
decresc. / dim. (with optional dashed continuation line)
Gradual dynamic text (poco a poco cresc., etc.)
Dynamic aligned to note vs. offset
Slur endpoint positioning (notehead side vs. stem side)
Portamento (smooth pitch slide, distinct from glissando)
Arpeggiate up (wavy line with upward arrow)
Arpeggiate down (wavy line with downward arrow)
String number indication (I, II, III, IV)
Artificial harmonic (diamond notehead for touched pitch)
Pedal line continuation across system/page break
Finger substitution (e.g., 2-1 on a held note)
Right-hand fingering (p, i, m, a, c)
Barré marking (full or partial, with fret)
Près de la table (near the soundboard)
Mute type (straight, cup, harmon, plunger, etc.)
Let ring / laissez vibrer (cymbal, triangle, etc.)
Automatic stem direction (based on note position on staff)
Stem direction override (force up or down)
Stem direction policy for voice (e.g., voice 1 up, voice 2 down)
Explicit stem length for a note/chord
Breve notehead (with or without vertical lines)
4-shape system (Sacred Harp: triangle, circle, square, diamond)
Hidden notehead (stem and other markings still visible)
Filled/open override (e.g., filled half note for white mensural)
Basic beaming (encoding the fact that notes are beamed together)
Explicit beam encoding vs. auto-beaming from time signature
Auto beaming grouped by beat (e.g., 4/4 = four groups)
Auto beaming for compound meter (e.g., 6/8 = two groups of three)
Auto beaming for irregular/asymmetric timesigs (e.g., 5/8 as 3+2 or 2+3)
Auto beaming of secondary beam breaks within a beam group
Individual notes crossing staff within a beam group
Gap between primary and secondary beam lines
Feathered beam line count (start and end number of beams)
Duplet (2 in the space of 3, in compound meter)
Arbitrary tuplet ratio (n in the space of m)
Tuplet with mixed note values (e.g., quarter + eighth in a triplet)
Tuplet split across separate beam groups
Tuplet with notes in different voices
Tuplet number with graphical note value (e.g., "3♪")
Tuplet bracket shape (straight lines with hook, curved)
Auto-hide bracket when tuplet is fully beamed
Proportional notation (horizontal spacing proportional to duration)
Spatial/graphic notation (time expressed by horizontal position)
Unmeasured / free rhythm (no barlines, no fixed meter)
Whole rest used as full-measure rest (regardless of time signature)
Old-style longa/breve rests for multi-measure rests
Staff position override (rest moved to specific line/space)
Rest offset for multiple voices (voice 1 up, voice 2 down)
Hidden rest (occupies time but not displayed)
Arbitrary clef sign on arbitrary staff line
Initial clef (start of piece / start of each system)
Clef change at start of bar
Mid-bar clef change (at specific rhythmic position)
Courtesy clef at end of system (warning before break)
Major sharp keys (G, D, A, E, B, F♯, C♯)
Major flat keys (F, B♭, E♭, A♭, D♭, G♭, C♭)
Minor keys (mode distinction from relative major)
No sharps or flats (C major / A minor)
Custom key signature (arbitrary accidentals on arbitrary pitches)
Mixed key signature (sharps and flats together)
Microtonal key signature (quarter-tone or other accidentals)
Modal key signatures (e.g., D Dorian with no accidentals vs. B♭)
Non-Western key signatures (Turkish, Arabic maqam, etc.)
Key encoded as number of fifths (e.g., -1 = F major)
Key encoded as explicit list of pitch/accidental pairs
Mode attribute (major, minor, dorian, mixolydian, etc.)
Cancellation naturals (naturals shown before new key signature)
Change to open/atonal (cancellation naturals only, no new key)
Courtesy key signature at end of system (warning before break)
Courtesy cancellation naturals at end of system
Transposed key signature (written key for transposing instrument)
Atonal transposing instrument (no key signature, but transposition applies)
Single-number time signature (e.g., 3 alone, common in early music)
Open / free time (no time signature, unmeasured)
Alternating meters (e.g., 3/4 + 4/4 shown together, bars alternate)
Interchangeable meters (e.g., 2/4 ≈ 6/8, bars may use either)
Different time signatures on different staves simultaneously
Local time signature (applies to one staff only, not full score)
Courtesy time signature at end of system (warning before break)
Time signature cancellation (revert to no time signature)
Proportio signs (numeric ratios for proportional tempo changes)
Symbol style: C and cut-C vs. numeric 4/4 and 2/2
Large time signature spanning multiple staves
Abbreviated time signature (numerator only for changes)
Time signature with graphical note denominator (e.g., 3/♪ instead of 3/8)
Metric modulation with dotted note values
Tempo equation across time signature change
Tick (short line at top of staff)
Barline spanning a staff group (e.g., all strings)
Barline spanning a subset of staves
Barline only between staves, not through (Mensurstrich)
Barline on a single staff only
Barline connecting grand staff (e.g., piano, organ)
Hidden barline (barline exists semantically but not drawn)
Gap between barline components (e.g., space in double barline)
Multiple voices on a single staff (voice 1, 2, 3, 4+)
Voice identifier (numeric, named, or arbitrary ID)
Strategy for unison noteheads with same duration (single notehead with two stems vs. separate adjacent noteheads)
Strategy for unison noteheads with different durations
Voice hiding (muting a voice from display)
Arbitrary number of staff lines, with definition of how to intrepret
Number of visual repeat dots (2 is standard; 4 seen in older editions)
Open-ended volta (no closing barline, e.g., final ending)
Volta bracket continuation across system break
Single-beat repeat (slash on beat, repeats previous beat)
Two-beat repeat (slash with dot, repeats previous two beats)
Single-bar repeat (% sign, repeats previous measure)
Repeat count above simile marks (e.g., "4" above % signs)
Multi-bar simile split across system break
Segno/coda placement (above staff, at barline, etc.)
Fine marking (end point after D.C. or D.S.)
Double-letter rehearsal marks (AA, BB, etc., after Z)
Rehearsal mark font, size, and style
Section name (Intro, Verse, Chorus, Bridge, Coda, etc.)
Section boundary (semantic start/end of a named section)
Movement or act designation within a single score
Movement break (new movement starts on same page vs. new page)
Measure number display frequency (every bar, every N bars, at system start)
Measure number override (custom number at specific point)
Measure number offset (e.g., pickup bar = 0 or unnumbered)
Measure number font, size, and style
Measure number placement (above staff, below, at barline)
Inhibited system break (prevent break at this barline)
Headers and footers (title, composer, page number, copyright)
Distance between staves within a system
System barline grouping (which staves barlines connect)
Ability to encode multiple (alternative) layouts for the same underlying music
Ability to choose explicit parts to include in a system
Ability to choose explicit voices to include in a system
Ability to choose explicit staffs (from multi-staff instruments) to include in a system
Ability to set stem direction for notes in a given system+source
Show all staves on first system even if empty
Hide specific staff in specific systems
Ossia staff scope (spans specific measures only)
Cue staff (small staff showing another instrument's part)
Metronome mark with dotted note value (♩. = 80)
Combined tempo text and metronome mark (Allegro ♩ = 132)
Gradual tempo text (poco a poco rit., etc.)
Dashed continuation line for gradual tempo changes
Tempo mark placement (above staff, above system)
Tempo text font, size, and style
Straight feel (return to even eighths after swing)
Sixteenth-note swing (shuffle feel on sixteenths instead of eighths)
Text frame / text block (title page, program notes)
Instrument name display preference (e.g., first system full, subsequent abbreviated)
Number of staves per instrument (1, 2 for piano, 3 for organ)
Melisma (one syllable sustained across multiple notes)
Elision / synalepha (two syllables merged on one note, ‿)
Lyric attached to rest (spoken text, recitative)
Lyric on chord (single syllable for multi-note event)
Multiple syllables on one note (parlando, rapid text)
Hyphen between syllables of a word
Multiple hyphens for wide note spacing
Melisma extender line (underscore through held syllable)
Extender line endpoint (ends at last note of melisma)
Extender line continuation across system break
Word extension line (after final syllable held through notes)
Verse label displayed before first syllable ("1.", "V2", etc.)
Font / style per lyric line (e.g., italic for translations)
Spoken text / stage direction within lyric lines
Added tone (add9, add11, add13 - no implied lower extensions)
Slash chord / bass note (e.g., C/E, Am/G)
Non-chord-tone bass (e.g., C/F, bass note not in the chord)
Polychord (e.g., C over D♭, stacked chord symbols)
Pedal bass indication (repeated bass note across chord changes)
Chord repeat slash (rhythm slashes indicating previous chord continues)
Semantic encoding (root, quality, extensions as structured data)
Interval-based encoding (list of pitch classes / semitone offsets)
Symbol style system (jazz, pop, classical, Nashville)
Superscript layout for extensions and alterations
Chord symbol font, size, and style
Region markers (chord region with rhythm slashes)
Starting fret number (for chords above first position)
Inline diagram above staff (aligned to chord symbol position)
Diamond notation (whole note, hold chord for full bar)
Roman numeral (I-VII, uppercase major / lowercase minor)
Bass figures (numbers indicating intervals above bass)
Slashed figure (slash through number indicating raised tone)
Line order (highest pitch at top vs. bottom)
Standard tuning definition (E A D G B E, etc.)
Alternate tuning (drop D, open G, DADGAD, etc.)
Tuning displayed at start of tablature
Tuning changes in the middle of a piece
Fret number on string (basic tablature note)
Rhythm-less tablature (fret numbers only, no stems)
Rhythm shown on paired standard notation staff only
Tablature as standalone staff (no paired notation)
Shift slide (legato slide from one fret to another)
Slide in from above/below (approach slide, no origin fret)
Slide out to above/below (exit slide, no target fret)
Right-hand fingering (p, i, m, a, c)
Unison bend (bend one string to match pitch of another)
Bend arrow notation (arrow with interval text)
Let ring (dashed line or tied notes)
Whammy bar / tremolo bar dive and return
Whammy bar dip (quick dive and return)
Stem position (above staff, through staff, or below)
